content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
' 428 ,J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
( 916) 322-5660 • Fax ( 916) 322-0886 
Tom Cackette 
4728 Crestwood Way 
Sacramento CA 95822 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-167 
Dear Mr. Cackette: 
November 14, 2016 
This letter responds lo your request for advice regarding lobbying provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the "Act").1 
QUESTIONS 
1. Must you register as a lobbyist when a client pays you less than $2,000 a month to 
prepare a report that may be used in the future by your client to advocate for adoption of 
a regulation by the Air Resources Board ("ARB") and you may be hired to help explain 
the report to other non-governmental organizations ("NGOs) and the Air Resources 
Board ("ARB") staff? 
2. If you receive more than $2,000 per month for lobbying services when combining 
payments from multiple clients for different projects will you have lo register as a 
lobbyist? 
CONCLUSION 
I. No. If you do not receive $2,000 or more in a month for lobbying you will not have to 
register as a lobbyist. 
2. Yes. If you receive $2,000 or more per month for lobbying services, even if no one 
client has paid $2,000 or more you will have to register as a lobbyist. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references arc to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission arc contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Tille 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references arc to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-16-167 
Page No. 2 
You are a self-employed consultant and you hope to be hired by an NGO to write a report 
evaluating future greenhouse gas emission standards for new passenger cars that could in the future 
be adopted by the ARB. You would not be engaged with the ARB during preparation of the report. 
This report may be used in the future by your client to advocate for adoption of a regulation by the 
ARB. Once the report is finished you may be hired by the NGO to help it explain the report to other 
NGOs and the ARB staff in meetings arranged by your client. At these meetings, which you may 
attend, your client may advocate that the ARB adopt a specific admission standard, in part based on 
your report. Your monthly income from this client for these services would be less than $2,000 per 
month. 
You may also have a different client hire you to discuss other possible regulations on a 
completely unrelated subject such as diesel fuel regulations for heavy trucks (whereas the other 
client would have you working on tailpipe emissions for cars). This client would also be paying you 
less than $2,000 a month for these services. When combined you would be receiving over $2,000 a 
month from these clients. 
ANALYSIS 
The word "lobbying" is a common expression for "influencing legislative or administrative 
action." The Act defines "influencing legislative or administrative action" as promoting, supporting, 
influencing, modifying, opposing or delaying any legislative or administrative action by any means, 
including but not limited to the provision or use of information, statistics, studies, or analyses. 
(Section 82032.) 
A lobbyist is defined in Section 82039 as: 
"[A]ny individual who receives two thousand dollars ($ 2,000) or more in economic 
consideration in a calendar month, other than reimbursement for reasonable travel 
expenses, or whm,e principal duties as an employee are, to communicate directly or 
through his or her agents with any elective state official, agency official, or legislative 
official for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action." 
Section 82045 defines "payments to influence legislative or administrative action" as followÅ: 
"(a) Direct or indirect payment to a lobbyist whether for salary, fee, compensation 
for expenses, or any other purpose, by a person employing or contracting for the 
services of the lobbyist separately or jointly with other persons; 
"(b) Payment in support or assistance of a lobbyist or his activities, including but 
not limited to the direct payment of expenses incurred at the request or suggestion 
of the lobbyist; 
"(c) Payment which directly or indirectly benefits any elective state official, 
legislative official or agency official or a member of the immediate family of any 
such official; 


File No. A-16-167 
Page No. 3 
"(d) Payment, including compensation, payment or reimbursement for the 
services, time or expenses of an employee, for or in connection with direct 
communication with any elective state official, legislative official or agency 
official; 
"(e) Payment for or in connection with soliciting or urging other persons to enter 
into direct communication with any elective state official, legislative official or 
agency official. " 
"Compensation" is defined in Regulation 18239(d)(2) as " . . .  any economic consideration, 
other than reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses, i.e., expenses for transportation plus a 
reasonable sum for food and lodging." 
Regulation 18239(d)(3) defines the term "direct communication" as" . . .  appearing as a 
witness before, talking to (either by telephone or in person), corresponding with, or answering 
questions or inquiries from, any qualifying official, either personally or through an agent who acts 
under one's direct supervision, control or direction." 
When applying the compensation test, an individual or entity must aggregate payments for 
preparation work if tile ll'ork is u11dertake11 for the purpose of engaging i11 direct co111111w1icatio11. 
We have previously advised that such work may include research, (including legal research), 
conducting studies, writing correspondence, developing testimony, attending hearings or meetings 
and drafting legislation. ( Cfretello-Joy Advice Letter, No. 08-129; Surto11 Advice Letter, No. 1-99-
093; Pessner Advice Letter No. 1-93-268.) You have stated you will be hired to produce a report or 
reports that may later be used for direct communication with qualifying officials by you and others. 
If you are hired to produce such reports for the purpose of them being used during direct 
communication and you engage in direct communication, then the preparation work put into the 
reports would qualify as lobbying activity once it reaches the $2,000 threshold per calendar month. 
Additionally, if you had direct communication with a qualifying official and received 
payment of $2,000 or more in a calendar month, there is a rebuttable presumption affecting the 
burden of producing evidence that all compensation from that lobbyist to you during that calendar 
month is for direct communication (therefore qualifying as lobbying activity). This presumption can 
be rebutted by evidence that may include testimony, records, bills, and receipts establishing the 
payments made to you and the goodÆ and services provided. 
If payment for the work you have described, including preparation work undertaken for the 
purpose of engaging in direct communication, does not meet the monetary threshold, any 
compensation allocable to this work is properly excluded from the calculation and you are not 
required to file as a lobbyist. However, if you do reach the monetary threshold when combining the 
total income for lobbying services from all of your clients you will have to register as a lobbyist. 
For example, you have described that you may have two clients that each pay you less than $2,000 
per month for unrelated lobbying services, but when those two payments are combined they are 
over $2,000 per month. In this case you would be required to register as a lobbyist. 


File No. A-16-167 
Page No. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
SKB:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Sukhi K. Brar 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

I. No. If you do not receive $2,000 or more in a month for lobbying you will not have to

client has paid $2,000 or more you will have to register as a lobbyist. 
The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission arc contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Tille 2 of the California Code of Regulations.
